title: SEGMENT 18 — Synthesis: what the microbiome era has actually delivered

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Synthesis: what the microbiome era has actually delivered

We've covered a lot of ground in the past two hours, and I want to use this last segment to step back and synthesize what the microbiome era has actually delivered, what it has not delivered, and what to look for in the next decade.
Because there's a real risk, if you read too much breathless microbiome journalism, of either thinking the field has revolutionized medicine (it mostly hasn't yet) or that it's all hype (it's not).
The truth is more interesting and more nuanced.
What has the microbiome era delivered, unambiguously?
First, a revised picture of what an animal is.

The realization that humans (and all other animals) are holobionts, half microbial by cell count, with microbial communities that perform essential metabolic and immunological functions for the host, is a permanent change to biology.

The pre-microbiome view of ourselves as essentially sterile organisms that occasionally got infections was wrong, and the corrected view has reshaped textbooks, medical education, and basic biological thinking.
This is not a small thing, even if it doesn't directly cure any disease.
Second, a methodological revolution in microbiology.
Culture-free DNA sequencing has opened the unculturable majority.
The number of known bacterial and archaeal phyla has expanded enormously.
The microbial world we can characterize has gone from a tiny biased slice to a vast catalog that grows every year.
The tools — 16S sequencing, shotgun metagenomics, single-cell genomics, viromics, multi-omics — are mature, accessible, and continually improving.
Microbiology before metagenomics and microbiology after metagenomics are different fields.
Third, FMT for C. diff.
A genuine, robust, FDA-approved therapy that demonstrates microbiome composition can causally affect disease and that microbial communities can be transferred between people with therapeutic effect.
One disease, one treatment, but a real proof of principle.
Fourth, microbiome biomarkers for a few specific clinical applications.
Colorectal cancer screening from stool.
Cancer immunotherapy response prediction.
C. diff recurrence risk.
These are early but real translational wins.
Fifth, a vast discovery engine for new biology.
CRISPR-Cas13.
Teixobactin.
Novel enzymes from extreme environments.
The metagenome is one of the most productive sources of new biological tools and molecules we have ever had access to, and that productivity will continue.
What has the microbiome era not yet delivered, despite confident promises?

It has not delivered transformative treatments for the major chronic diseases that microbiome composition is correlated with — obesity, IBD, depression, autism, metabolic syndrome — beyond the C. diff case.
The correlations are real but mostly weak, and the causal interventions have so far underperformed the early hype.
It has not delivered routine, individualized microbiome-based medicine.
There is no widely accepted clinical test you take that profiles your microbiome and tells your doctor what to do about it.
The diagnostic and prognostic uses of microbiome data are mostly still in research mode.
It has not delivered probiotics that actually do what they're advertised to do.
Most over-the-counter probiotic products have weak or absent evidence for the claims on their labels.
The probiotics industry, which is enormous, is largely running on aspirational marketing rather than rigorous clinical evidence for most products.

Some specific probiotic strains for specific indications (like Saccharomyces boulardii for antibiotic-associated diarrhea, or specific Bifidobacterium strains for infant colic) have decent evidence; the vast majority don't.
It has not yet delivered on the engineered microbiome therapeutics promise at scale.
Vowst is a real product.
The next ones are still in trials, with uncertain outcomes.
What should you watch for in the next decade?
Larger, better-controlled, multi-cohort studies that distinguish real microbiome-disease associations from artifacts.
The field has gone through its catalog phase and its early-correlation phase; the next phase is the rigorous replication and intervention phase.

Look for studies that show effects across multiple independent cohorts, that test causality with FMT or humanized-mouse experiments, that integrate multi-omics for mechanism, that use modern causal inference methods.
Maturation of the microbiome therapeutics industry.
The early companies have struggled; the next generation, building on lessons learned, may or may not do better.
Watch for clinical readouts from Vedanta, Seres's pipeline beyond Vowst, the engineered-bacteria companies, and academic-spinout startups with more focused indications.
Integration of microbiome data into routine clinical decision-making for specific indications, especially cancer immunotherapy, colorectal cancer screening, and C. diff prevention.
The technical and validation work is mostly done; the regulatory and reimbursement work is the bottleneck.
Continued expansion of environmental and functional metagenomics.
New CRISPR systems, new antibiotics, new enzymes, new metabolic pathways will keep coming out of metagenomic mining.
The microbial world is enormous and underexplored, and the discovery engine has decades of productivity ahead.
Better understanding of the gut-brain axis and other host-microbe signaling axes.
The mechanistic biology is genuinely interesting, even if the therapeutic translation lags.
Expect significant advances in understanding how specific microbial metabolites act on specific host receptors and pathways.
And, frankly, more humility from the field about what microbiome data can and cannot tell us.
The first wave of microbiome science was exuberant and sometimes overstated its conclusions.
The second wave should be more careful, more replicable, and more honest about uncertainty.
That's a normal trajectory for any field that goes from a methodological revolution to a mature clinical science.
Here is the picture I want you to leave with.
The microbiome is a vast, ancient, planetary phenomenon that DNA sequencing finally let us see.
The human microbiome is a tiny part of it that matters for human health in ways we are only beginning to map.

Metagenomics is the foundational tool that unlocked all of this, and it will continue to drive the field forward — through better sequencing, better assembly, better multi-omics integration, better causal inference, and better translation.
We are perhaps twenty years into a hundred-year project to understand and harness the microbial world.
The first twenty years have given us a methodological revolution, a reframing of what we are, one major therapy, and a discovery pipeline.
The next eighty years will give us the rest.
Go read papers.
Be skeptical of headlines.
Look at the methods.
Look at the effect sizes.
Look at the replication.
The field needs more rigorous practitioners, and that's where some of you should aim.
Thanks for your attention.
I'm happy to take questions.
